questions.passage:
The unreliable narrator is often treated as a locked cabinet. Readers gather discrepancies, compare alibis, and wait for the hidden version of events to emerge. Once the narrator’s errors have been catalogued, the cabinet is declared open and the novel solved. This habit mistakes unreliability for a defect in transmission. In the strongest works, unreliability is not a veil over the real story; it is the form through which the story becomes morally legible.

Consider the difference between factual correction and ethical understanding. A narrator may misstate a date, suppress a motive, or exaggerate another character’s cruelty. External evidence can expose each distortion. Yet a corrected chronology does not explain why the narrator requires the distortion, what kind of self must be preserved by it, or how other people are reduced to supporting roles in that preservation. The lie matters less as a missing fact than as an action performed upon the world.

This is why the language of puzzles is so limiting. A puzzle rewards the person who stands outside it. The pieces are inert, the solution stable, and the solver unchanged by success. Narrative unreliability often denies that clean position. We depend on the narrator for access even while learning to distrust the account. Tone, selection, and rhythm continue to shape our sympathies after particular claims have failed. We cannot simply discard the voice and retain a neutral sequence of events, because the sequence was never given independently of the voice.

The dependence can become uncomfortable. Readers sometimes complain that a novel has “tricked” them into caring for someone later exposed as vain, cruel, or self-deceived. But this complaint grants fiction a strange duty: characters must disclose their moral status before soliciting attention. In ordinary life, attention is rarely offered under such conditions. We understand others through partial testimony, strategic silence, and our own desire to believe. An unreliable narrator does not merely imitate this uncertainty. The form recruits our habits of trust and lets us observe them under pressure.

None of this means that facts are irrelevant. If every contradiction could be dismissed as expressive, unreliability would become an excuse for carelessness. The factual structure must remain precise enough for distortions to have direction. A narrator who alters a memory to avoid shame differs from one who invents at random. The reader needs grounds for distinguishing concealment, confusion, fantasy, and deliberate manipulation. But these distinctions matter because they describe modes of relation, not because they lead to a final transcript purified of perspective.

The finest unreliable narratives therefore end with a residue that no revelation removes. We may know what happened and remain uncertain how to distribute responsibility. We may reject the narrator’s judgment while recognizing the need that produced it. We may even discover that our eagerness to expose deception was another way of avoiding dependence on a compromised voice. The narrative has not failed to yield a solution. It has shown why solution is an impoverished name for understanding.

To read such a work as a puzzle is to seek release from the very relation it constructs. The narrator’s account should be checked, but not merely escaped. Its fractures reveal how a person arranges reality to remain inhabitable and how readily another person may consent to that arrangement. Unreliability is not simply a barrier between reader and truth. It is a drama of what truth costs when it must pass through a human voice.
questions.stem: The passage's main argument is that unreliable narration

choices.A: should conceal factual contradictions until the final pages of a novel.
choices.B: allows readers to reconstruct a neutral account beneath a deceptive voice.
choices.C: makes moral relations visible through the reader's dependence on a compromised account.
choices.D: works best when narrators deliberately manipulate every detail they present.

explanations.A: The author values precise contradictions but does not prescribe their timing or require a final revelation.
explanations.B: The passage denies that the narrative can be reduced to a perspective-free sequence of events.
explanations.C: Correct. Unreliability matters because the narrator’s distortions and the reader’s continued dependence expose habits of trust, self-preservation, and moral responsibility.
explanations.D: The author distinguishes deliberate manipulation from confusion, fantasy, and concealment rather than making it universal.

questions.stem: The comparison of an unreliable narrative to a puzzle is criticized because a puzzle

choices.A: contains too few clues to support ethical interpretation.
choices.B: permits a detached solution outside the problem.
choices.C: requires a stable chronology that fiction cannot provide.
choices.D: depends on deliberate deception rather than self-deception.

explanations.A: The criticism concerns the relation between solver and material rather than the number of clues.
explanations.B: Correct. A puzzle imagines inert pieces and an unchanged solver, while unreliable narration implicates readers through continuing dependence on the voice they distrust.
explanations.C: The author accepts that factual structure can be precise enough to expose directed distortion.
explanations.D: Narrative unreliability includes several modes, and the puzzle comparison is not rejected because of one specific source of error.

questions.stem: Which claim is most strongly implied by the passage?

choices.A: Readers should withhold sympathy until a narrator's factual accuracy is established.
choices.B: A narrator's moral character can be inferred from any contradiction in the account.
choices.C: Factual inconsistencies matter only when the narrator intends to deceive.
choices.D: Correcting a false statement may leave its psychological purpose unexplained.

explanations.A: The passage considers how sympathy can precede moral certainty and rejects a duty of advance disclosure.
explanations.B: Contradictions require interpretation and can arise from shame, confusion, fantasy, or manipulation.
explanations.C: The author assigns significance to self-deception and confusion as well as deliberate lying.
explanations.D: Correct. Establishing the accurate date or motive does not reveal why the narrator needed the distortion or what self-image it protected.

questions.stem: A critic publishes a timeline correcting every factual error in a novel narrated by a self-deceived character. The author would most likely respond that the timeline

choices.A: clarifies events but leaves the narrator's distortions unexplained.
choices.B: destroys the novel by replacing ambiguity with factual certainty.
choices.C: proves that the narrator was deliberately manipulative throughout.
choices.D: offers the only reliable basis for judging the other characters.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage welcomes factual checking but insists that correction alone cannot explain the narrator’s needs, relations, or effects on the reader.
explanations.B: The author does not claim that accurate reconstruction destroys the work; the objection is to treating reconstruction as sufficient.
explanations.C: Self-deception cannot be converted into deliberate manipulation merely by identifying errors.
explanations.D: Other characters remain mediated by the same narrative voice, so a chronology cannot supply a complete moral basis.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the author's account of reader dependence?

choices.A: Readers detect more contradictions when they reread a novel.
choices.B: Authors often revise narrators' timelines before publication.
choices.C: Readers retain emotional framing after rejecting factual claims.
choices.D: Novels with unreliable narrators contain more first-person pronouns.

explanations.A: Improved detection would show that contradictions can be found but not that readers remain shaped by the compromised voice.
explanations.B: Revision practices concern composition rather than the reader’s continued dependence on tone and selection.
explanations.C: Correct. Persistent emotional framing despite factual distrust directly supports the claim that readers cannot discard the voice and retain a neutral event sequence.
explanations.D: Pronoun frequency does not demonstrate how readers respond to a narrator they distrust.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward treating unreliable narrators as puzzles is best described as

choices.A: amused tolerance for a harmless reading practice.
choices.B: qualified approval when factual errors are numerous.
choices.D: firm rejection of an approach that evades moral dependence.

explanations.A: The language of “impoverished” understanding and attempted escape is critical rather than tolerant.
explanations.B: The author supports checking facts but rejects puzzle-solving as the governing model regardless of the number of errors.
explanations.C: The passage advances a definite account of how unreliability works and is not indifferent among interpretations.
explanations.D: Correct. The author argues that puzzle-solving seeks a detached release from the compromised relation through which the work gains moral force.
